Supported claims (directly from supplied KB):
- Purpose of tracing: ensure alignment between requirements and designs, manage effects of change, support scope/change/risk/time/cost/communication management, detect gaps and unneeded functionality [S1].
- Common relationship types to capture: derive, depends (necessity, effort), satisfy, validate [S2].
- Key repository and tooling guidance: use a traceability repository; tools scale with number of requirements and formality; can be as simple as text or a dedicated RM tool [S2].
- Useful requirement attributes to include for management and maintenance: absolute reference (unique ID), author, complexity, ownership, priority, source/other attributes planned during elicitation [S4].
- Stakeholders who should be involved or informed about traceability and who benefit from it: customers, domain SMEs, end users, implementation SME, operational support, project manager, sponsor, suppliers, tester [S3].
- Outcomes expected from traceability activities: requirements (traced) and designs (traced) so coverage and effects of change are identifiable [S3].

Practical Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — recommended structure and guidance
Purpose and audience
- Purpose: provide a maintained, searchable mapping of requirements to related items (higher-level needs, design elements, solution components, test cases, releases/iterations) to enable impact analysis, coverage verification, and change management [S1][S3].
- Intended audience: business analyst(s), project manager, testers, implementation SMEs, sponsors, domain SMEs, operational support [S3].

Recommended sections/columns (logical order) and what each should capture
1) Unique ID (Absolute reference)
- Unique, never-reused identifier for the requirement [S4].
2) Requirement statement / short name
- Clear, concise description of the requirement; must be consistently represented and easily understandable [S2][S3].
3) Requirement type / level
- Indicate business, stakeholder, solution, or transition requirement so lineage and abstraction level are explicit [S1].
4) Source / Author / Owner
- Who proposed/owns the requirement and contact for clarification (author and ownership support maintenance) [S4].
5) Priority and Complexity
- Priority (value or implementation sequence) and complexity to support planning and trade-offs [S4].
6) Relationships / Traces
- Backward traces (derived from which higher-level requirement/goal)
- Forward traces (satisfied by which design/solution components)
- Dependencies (necessity/effort) and derived relationships explicitly noted [S1][S2].
7) Satisfies (Solution component / design element)
- Link to component(s) that satisfy the requirement (e.g., module name, API, process step) to support allocation and release planning [S1][S2].
8) Validated by (Test case / acceptance criteria)
- Link to test case IDs or acceptance criteria used to validate the requirement (supports test planning) [S2][S3].
9) Release / Phase / Iteration
- Indicates planned or actual release/phase where requirement will be delivered (supports scope and release planning) [S1][S3].
10) Status and Change history
- Current status (e.g., proposed, approved, implemented) and brief change log to support maintenance and traceability over time [S3][S2].
11) Notes / Comments / Regulatory references
- Any legal/regulatory constraints or additional context that affect tracing rules or reuse [S2][S4].

Required inputs and supporting evidence
- Elicited requirements and designs (goals, business/stakeholder/solution/transition requirements) to populate rows and lineage [S1][S3].
- Information management approach or plan (defines traceability approach and formality) [S2].
- Legal/regulatory information when applicable to define traceability rules [S2].
- Access to requirements repository or tool (text document or RM tool) to store and maintain traces [S2].

Assumptions, dependencies, constraints, and exclusions (evidence-backed vs suggested)
- Evidence-backed assumptions/dependencies:
  - Level of formality should reflect value vs overhead; tracing effort grows with number of requirements and formality [S1][S2].
  - Repository/tool selection depends on scale and organizational standards; a simple document may suffice for small sets, dedicated tools for large sets [S2][S4].
- Suggested placeholders (not specified in supplied KB):
  - Specific tool names, naming conventions, or column data types — evidence in KB describes concept but does not prescribe tool or exact schema [S2][S4].

Review, validation, approval, and sign-off checkpoints
- Peer review of mapping (BA ↔ SMEs) to verify correctness of traces and relationships [S3].
- Sponsor or owner approval of critical relationships (sponsors are required to approve various relationships) [S3].
- Tester validation that all requirements have associated test cases (tester needs traceability when creating test plans) [S3][S2].
- Project manager checkpoint to confirm RTM supports change and scope management [S3].

Traceability and quality checks
- Coverage check: every requirement should have backward or forward trace(s) to at least one related element (goal, design, or test) to detect missing functionality or implemented-but-unsupported functionality [S1].
- Consistency check: ensure attributes (owner, priority, status) are present and up-to-date to support maintenance [S3][S4].
- Dependency analysis: inspect “depends” relationships for necessity or effort dependencies to guide sequencing and release planning [S2].
- Regular maintenance: maintain relationships and attributes as requirements change and through the solution lifecycle [S3].

Maintenance and reuse guidance
- Maintain requirements with consistent naming and approved processes, keep repository access controlled and searchable to support reuse [S3][S4].
- Plan for reuse candidate requirements (regulatory, contractual, SLAs, business rules) and store them for future initiatives [S4].

Unresolved questions and evidence gaps (items the supplied KB does not fully specify)
- Exact column data formats, naming conventions, and example templates are not provided in the supplied excerpts; these must be defined per project/tool and organizational standards (evidence: KB explains attributes and repository options but does not prescribe a specific template) [S2][S4].
- Specific tool selection criteria and migration steps between simple and complex repositories are not detailed in the supplied texts; select tooling based on scale and the information management approach [S2].

Quick implementation checklist (practical steps)
1. Define traceability approach and level of formality with stakeholders and information management plan [S2].
2. Select repository/tool appropriate to scale and standards [S2][S4].
3. Create RTM columns as recommended above, at minimum: Unique ID, statement, type, source/owner, priority, relationships, satisfied-by, validated-by, release, status [S4][S1][S2].
4. Populate RTM from elicited requirements and designs; link test cases and solution components [S1][S3].
5. Run coverage and dependency checks; review with SMEs, testers, project manager, sponsor [S3][S2].
6. Maintain RTM through change control and use it for impact analysis and release planning [S1][S3].

Evidence citations (first occurrence order): [S1], [S2], [S3], [S4].

A message is shown as an arrow coming from the activation box of the object that sends the message to theSequence Diagramsactivation box of the object that receives the message.The name of the message is placed on top of the arrowed line. There are different types of messages:• Synchronous Call: transfers control to the receiving object. PurposeStakeholder lists, maps, and personas assist the business analyst in analyzing stakeholders and their characteristics. DescriptionStakeholder analysis involves identifying the stakeholders that may be affected by a proposed initiative or that share a common business need. details on the work involved in conducting a thorough stakeholder analysis, see Plan Stakeholder Engagement (p. 31).When analyzing stakeholders, business analysts utilize one or more techniques to draw out a list of stakeholders and analyze them. Stakeholder lists, maps, and personas are three tools that can be utilized when conducting this work.10.43.3
Elements.1 Stakeholder ListsA business analyst may apply a number of techniques to generate a stakeholder list. Brainstorming and interviews are two common techniques that can be used.
The goal is to ensure a thorough list is produced because this list is central to both stakeholder analysis activities and the planning work the business analyst performs for elicitation, collaboration, and communication.Stakeholder lists may become quite lengthy. As the analysis is conducted, the business analyst categorizes and adds structure to the list. It is important to have an exhaustive list to ensure that no important stakeholder or stakeholder group has been overlooked, which opens up the risk that requirements will be missed later on.Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas.2 Stakeholder MapStakeholder maps are diagrams that depict the relationship of stakeholders to the solution and to one another.• Stakeholder Matrix: maps the level of stakeholder influence against the level of stakeholder interest.• Onion Diagram: indicates how involved the stakeholders are with the solution, which stakeholders will directly interact with the solution or participate in a business process, which are part of the larger organization, and which are outside the organization.The business analyst typically starts their stakeholder analysis by reviewing the proposed scope of the solution and then analyzing which groups will be impacted. At the start of this analysis, the business analyst may produce a stakeholder matrix to identify each stakeholder and their role as it pertains to the development of the requirements. Throughout a project, a stakeholder’s position on the matrix can change due to organizational, environmental, or requirement scope changes. Due to these potential changes, stakeholder analysis is considered iterative and reviewed frequently by the business analyst.Figure 10.43.1: Stakeholder Matrixremains satisfied.and support the change.StakeholderKeep informed; stakeholder is likely to be very concernedand may feel anxious aboutMonitor to ensure stakeholders interest or influence do not change.lack of control.LowStakeholder• High Influence/High Impact: the stakeholders are key players in the change effort. The business analyst should focus their efforts and engage this group regularly.• High Influence/Low Impact: the stakeholders have needs that should be met. The business analyst should engage and consult with them, while also attempting to engage them and increase their level of interest with the change activity.• Low Influence/High Impact: the stakeholders are supporters of and potential goodwill ambassadors for the change effort. The business analyst should engage this group for their input and show interest in their needs.Techniques• Low Influence/Low Impact: the stakeholders can be kept informed using general communications. Genuine stakeholder engagement requires that all stakeholders involved feel that they are heard, their opinions matter, and their contributions are recognized.
Collaboration involves regular, frequent, and bi-directional communication.
Collaborative relationships help maintain the free flow of information when obstacles and setbacks occur, and promote a shared effort to resolve problems and achieve desired outcomes.Elicitation and Collaboration4.5.5
Guidelines and Tools• Business Analysis Approach: describes the nature and level of collaboration required from each stakeholder group to perform planned business analysis activities.• Business Objectives: describe the desired direction needed to achieve the future state. They can be used to focus diverse stakeholders on a common vision of the desired business outcomes.• Future State Description: defines the desired future state and the expected value it delivers which can be used to focus diverse stakeholders on the common goal.• Recommended Actions: communicating what should be done to improve the value of a solution can help to galvanize support and focus stakeholders on a common goal.• Risk Analysis Results: stakeholder-related risks will need to be addressed to ensure stakeholder collaboration activities are successful.4.5.6
Techniques• Collaborative Games: used to stimulate teamwork and collaboration by temporarily immersing participants in a safe and fun situation in which they can share their knowledge and experience on a given topic, identify hidden assumptions, and explore that knowledge in ways that may not occur during the course of normal interactions.• Lessons Learned: used to understand stakeholders' satisfaction or dissatisfaction, and offer them an opportunity to help improve the working relationships.• Risk Analysis and Management: used to identify and manage risks as they relate to stakeholder involvement, participation, and engagement.• Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas: used to determine who is available to participate in the business analysis work, show the informal relationships between stakeholders, and understand which stakeholders should be consulted about different kinds of business analysis information.4.5.7
Stakeholders• All stakeholders: all types of stakeholders who might be involved in collaboration during change.4.5.8
Outputs• Stakeholder Engagement: willingness from stakeholders to engage in business analysis activities and interact with the business analyst when necessary.5
Requirements Life Cycle ManagementThe Requirements Life Cycle Management knowledge area describes the tasks that business analysts perform in order to manage and maintain requirements and design information from inception to retirement. These tasks describe establishing meaningful relationships between related requirements and designs, assessing changes to requirements and designs when changes are proposed, and analyzing and gaining consensus on changes.The purpose of requirements life cycle management is to ensure that business, stakeholder, and solution requirements and designs are aligned to one another and that the solution implements them. Elements.1 Stakeholder ListsA business analyst may apply a number of techniques to generate a stakeholder list. Brainstorming and interviews are two common techniques that can be used.
The goal is to ensure a thorough list is produced because this list is central to both stakeholder analysis activities and the planning work the business analyst performs for elicitation, collaboration, and communication.Stakeholder lists may become quite lengthy. As the analysis is conducted, the business analyst categorizes and adds structure to the list. It is important to have an exhaustive list to ensure that no important stakeholder or stakeholder group has been overlooked, which opens up the risk that requirements will be missed later on.Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas.2 Stakeholder MapStakeholder maps are diagrams that depict the relationship of stakeholders to the solution and to one another.• Stakeholder Matrix: maps the level of stakeholder influence against the level of stakeholder interest.• Onion Diagram: indicates how involved the stakeholders are with the solution, which stakeholders will directly interact with the solution or participate in a business process, which are part of the larger organization, and which are outside the organization.The business analyst typically starts their stakeholder analysis by reviewing the proposed scope of the solution and then analyzing which groups will be impacted. At the start of this analysis, the business analyst may produce a stakeholder matrix to identify each stakeholder and their role as it pertains to the development of the requirements. Throughout a project, a stakeholder’s position on the matrix can change due to organizational, environmental, or requirement scope changes. Due to these potential changes, stakeholder analysis is considered iterative and reviewed frequently by the business analyst.Figure 10.43.1: Stakeholder Matrixremains satisfied.and support the change.StakeholderKeep informed; stakeholder is likely to be very concernedand may feel anxious aboutMonitor to ensure stakeholders interest or influence do not change.lack of control.LowStakeholder• High Influence/High Impact: the stakeholders are key players in the change effort. The business analyst should focus their efforts and engage this group regularly.• High Influence/Low Impact: the stakeholders have needs that should be met. The business analyst should engage and consult with them, while also attempting to engage them and increase their level of interest with the change activity.• Low Influence/High Impact: the stakeholders are supporters of and potential goodwill ambassadors for the change effort. The business analyst should engage this group for their input and show interest in their needs.Techniques• Low Influence/Low Impact: the stakeholders can be kept informed using general communications. Genuine stakeholder engagement requires that all stakeholders involved feel that they are heard, their opinions matter, and their contributions are recognized.
Collaboration involves regular, frequent, and bi-directional communication.
Collaborative relationships help maintain the free flow of information when obstacles and setbacks occur, and promote a shared effort to resolve problems and achieve desired outcomes.Elicitation and Collaboration4.5.5
Guidelines and Tools• Business Analysis Approach: describes the nature and level of collaboration required from each stakeholder group to perform planned business analysis activities.• Business Objectives: describe the desired direction needed to achieve the future state. They can be used to focus diverse stakeholders on a common vision of the desired business outcomes.• Future State Description: defines the desired future state and the expected value it delivers which can be used to focus diverse stakeholders on the common goal.• Recommended Actions: communicating what should be done to improve the value of a solution can help to galvanize support and focus stakeholders on a common goal.• Risk Analysis Results: stakeholder-related risks will need to be addressed to ensure stakeholder collaboration activities are successful.4.5.6
Techniques• Collaborative Games: used to stimulate teamwork and collaboration by temporarily immersing participants in a safe and fun situation in which they can share their knowledge and experience on a given topic, identify hidden assumptions, and explore that knowledge in ways that may not occur during the course of normal interactions.• Lessons Learned: used to understand stakeholders' satisfaction or dissatisfaction, and offer them an opportunity to help improve the working relationships.• Risk Analysis and Management: used to identify and manage risks as they relate to stakeholder involvement, participation, and engagement.• Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas: used to determine who is available to participate in the business analysis work, show the informal relationships between stakeholders, and understand which stakeholders should be consulted about different kinds of business analysis information.4.5.7
Stakeholders• All stakeholders: all types of stakeholders who might be involved in collaboration during change.4.5.8
Outputs• Stakeholder Engagement: willingness from stakeholders to engage in business analysis activities and interact with the business analyst when necessary.5
Requirements Life Cycle ManagementThe Requirements Life Cycle Management knowledge area describes the tasks that business analysts perform in order to manage and maintain requirements and design information from inception to retirement. These tasks describe establishing meaningful relationships between related requirements and designs, assessing changes to requirements and designs when changes are proposed, and analyzing and gaining consensus on changes.The purpose of requirements life cycle management is to ensure that business, stakeholder, and solution requirements and designs are aligned to one another and that the solution implements them. response_text:
Direct answer

Purpose and audience
- Purpose: document who is affected by or can influence the initiative, characterize their authority/interest/attitudes, and prescribe engagement/collaboration/communication approaches to support requirements work and change success [S1][S2].
- Primary audience: business analysts, project/sponsor leadership, PMO, stakeholder-engagement owners, and subject-matter experts who will plan or execute elicitation, collaboration, and communications [S1][S3].

Recommended sections (logical order) with expected content/questions
1. Executive summary
   - Short description of why this stakeholder analysis was done and the key engagement decisions it supports (use initiative scope as driver) [S2].
2. Scope and assumptions
   - Which parts of the initiative were reviewed to identify stakeholders; assumptions about organizational boundaries, timebox of analysis, and exclusions (who/what was not considered) [S2].
3. Inputs and evidence sources (required source inputs)
   - Proposed solution scope and documentation, org charts, project charter, previous lessons learned, stakeholder interviews, brainstorming outputs, surveys/questionnaires, and subject-matter knowledge used to create the list and characterizations [S2][S5][S3].
4. Stakeholder inventory (exhaustive list)
   - A searchable list of stakeholders and stakeholder groups generated by brainstorming/interviews and other elicitation; each entry notes role, organization, contact/representation, and how they relate to scope (ensure an exhaustive list to reduce risk of missed requirements) [S1][S2].
5. Stakeholder characterizations and attributes
   - For each stakeholder/group capture: level of authority (decision-making), attitudes/interests toward the change, attitudes toward BA work/role, expected participation level, and criticality to approvals/deliverables [S1].
6. Stakeholder maps and visualizations (options)
   - Stakeholder matrix (interest vs influence) with guidance on interpretation and engagement implication for each quadrant [S2].
   - Onion diagram showing proximity/involvement with the solution (direct users, organization, external parties) [S2].
   - Relationship diagram showing inter‑stakeholder relationships where relevant [S2].
7. Roles & responsibility assignments (RACI or similar)
   - RACI matrix mapping stakeholders to major activities/artifacts (define R/A/C/I terms) and note any role conflicts or single-accountability rules (only one A) [S5].
8. Personas (where useful)
   - Research‑backed archetypes for key user groups: narrative goals, constraints, and typical interactions with the solution to guide design and requirements prioritization (clear note that personas are constructed from elicitation/surveys) [S5].
9. Engagement & collaboration recommendations
   - For each stakeholder/group recommend communication cadence, collaboration techniques (e.g., workshops, collaborative games), level of involvement required for BA tasks, and risks/mitigations to maintain participation [S3][S5].
10. Risks and dependency log (stakeholder-related)
   - Stakeholder engagement risks, their impact on elicitation and approvals, mitigation actions and owners; link to project risk register as needed [S3].
11. Monitoring and review plan
   - How stakeholder positions will be re-assessed (iteration frequency), metrics/indicators to watch (participation, attitude change, elicitation quality, timeliness of approvals), and triggers to update stakeholder artefacts [S2][S3].
12. Traceability and quality checks
   - Cross-reference table linking stakeholders to requirements, decisions, and approvals; validation checklist used to confirm stakeholder list completeness and persona evidence (source references: interview, survey, document) [S3].
13. Appendices
   - Raw interview notes, survey results, workshop artefacts, definitions of RACI terms, and legend for maps/diagrams [S5].

Required supporting evidence and artifacts to produce the deliverable
- Project scope/charter and any solution descriptions used to identify impacted groups [S2].
- Elicitation artifacts: interview transcripts, brainstorming outputs, surveys/questionnaires, workshops, and lessons-learned relevant to stakeholders [S1][S5][S3].
- Organizational structures (org charts) and decision‑making authority information to determine influence and accountability [S2].

Assumptions, dependencies, constraints, and exclusions (placeholders backed by evidence)
- Assumption: analysis begins from the documented proposed scope and is iterative; stakeholder positions can change over time so analysis will be reviewed periodically [S2].
- Dependency: requires access to subject matter experts and organizational documentation to produce exhaustive lists and personas [S1][S5].
- Constraint: time/effort trade-off — producing fully detailed personas or exhaustive maps may be costly; prioritize based on impact and project phase [S1].
- Exclusion note: if evidence for a stakeholder’s characteristics is missing, mark the entry as needing validation and do not assume attributes without elicitation [S1][S5].

Review, validation, and approval checkpoints
- Initial review: business analyst and project sponsor confirm stakeholder list completeness before detailed mapping (prevents missed requirement sources) [S2].
- Validation: circulate persona drafts and RACI to affected stakeholder representatives for confirmation (ensures narrative and responsibility assignments match reality) [S5].
- Approval/sign-off: sponsor or designated accountable stakeholder approves final stakeholder engagement plan and RACI assignments; record version and date of approval in header (stakeholder assignments may change later) [S5][S2].

Traceability and quality checks
- Map each stakeholder to requirements/decisions they influence or must approve; include source evidence (interview, document) for the assignment [S3].
- Use a checklist to confirm the stakeholder list used brainstorming/interviews and considered customers, suppliers, regulators, sponsors, SMEs, end users, help desk, and project team members [S2][S5].
- Track changes to stakeholder positions over time — treat stakeholder analysis as iterative with periodic reassessment [S2].

Unresolved questions and evidence gaps (items to resolve before finalizing deliverable)
- Any stakeholder entries lacking primary evidence (interview/survey/document) must be flagged for follow-up elicitation — do not infer attitudes or authority without source data [S1][S5].
- If the project lacks organizational decision‑authority information, the influence/interest placements in the stakeholder matrix are provisional and require confirmation [S2].

Options and trade-offs for practitioners
- Level of detail: produce a compact stakeholder matrix and RACI early to enable planning; expand to personas and full relationship diagrams where stakeholder impact is high or decisions depend on user behavior. This balances effort against risk of missed requirements [S1][S2][S5].
- Mapping technique choice: use interest vs influence matrix for engagement prioritization, onion diagram to show involvement layers, and RACI to define task responsibilities; combine as needed for clarity [S2][S5].

Evidence cited
- Elements, purpose and techniques for stakeholder lists, maps, and personas; need for exhaustive lists and iterative review [S1][S2][S5].
- Guidance on collaboration, monitoring engagement, and metrics/outputs to watch [S3].

RACI stands for the four types of responsibility that a stakeholder may hold on the initiative: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed. When completing a RACI matrix, it is important to ensure that all stakeholders or stakeholder groups have been identified. Further analysis is then conducted to assign the RACI designation in order to specify the level of responsibility expected from each stakeholder and/or group. It is common practice to define each term so that a consistent understanding of the assignment and associated roles are understood by any stakeholders utilizing the RACI matrix.• Responsible (R): the persons who will be performing the work on the task.• Accountable (A): the person who is ultimately held accountable for successful completion of the task and is the decision maker. Only one stakeholder receives this assignment.• Consulted (C): the stakeholder or stakeholder group who will be asked to provide an opinion or information about the task. This assignment is often provided to the subject matter experts (SMEs).• Informed (I): a stakeholder or stakeholder group that is kept up to date on the task and notified of its outcome. Informed is different from Consulted as with Informed the communication is one-direction (business analyst to stakeholder) and with Consulted the communication is two-way.Stakeholder List, Map, or PersonasFigure 10.43.3: RACI MatrixRACIARCCIICCCCRCCCR

I(varies).4 PersonasA persona is defined as a fictional character or archetype that exemplifies the way a typical user interacts with a product. Personas are helpful when there is a desire to understand the needs held by a group or class of users. Although the user groups are fictional, they are built to represent actual users. Research is conducted to understand the user group, and the personas are then created based upon knowledge rather than opinion. A number of elicitation techniques can be utilized to conduct this research. Interviews and surveys/questionnaires are two techniques commonly used to elicit this information. The persona is written in narrative form and focuses on providing insight into the goals of the group.
This allows the reader to see the story from the point of view of the stakeholder group. Personas help bring the user to life, which in turn makes the needs feel real to those who design and build solutions.10.43.4

Stakeholder Analysis — Deliverable Outline (for a new initiative)

Purpose and intended audience
- Purpose: to identify and analyse stakeholders who are affected by or can affect the initiative so decisions about engagement, collaboration and communications are targeted and reduce the risk of missed requirements or late objections [S1].
- Intended audience: project sponsor(s), business analyst(s), project manager, change/sponsorship team, key stakeholder representatives (executives, domain SMEs, user-group leads), and the project communications owner [S1][S2].

Recommended sections (logical order) and expected content
1. Cover / Identification
  - Title, initiative name, version, author, date, distribution list.
  - Note: the supplied knowledge base does not provide a template for metadata; include these fields as practical placeholders (evidence insufficient for a mandatory template).

2. Executive summary
  - Brief summary of overall stakeholder landscape, highest-risk stakeholder issues, and recommended next engagement actions based on analysis [S1][S2].

3. Scope and analysis approach
  - Describe scope of the stakeholder analysis (which parts of the solution/organization are in scope) and which BABOK-supported techniques were used to generate and analyse the list (e.g., brainstorming, interviews, surveys, stakeholder map, personas) [S1][S2][S5].
  - Indicate that stakeholder analysis is iterative and will be reviewed frequently as stakeholder positions can change due to organizational or scope changes [S2].

4. Stakeholder inventory (exhaustive list)
  - A structured list of stakeholder individuals and groups with role, organization, contact points, and initial classification (e.g., sponsor, SME, end user, regulator, supplier) — the business analyst should aim for an exhaustive list because omissions increase the risk of missing requirements [S2].

5. Stakeholder maps and visualisations
  - Stakeholder Matrix (influence vs interest): place each stakeholder/group to show who are key players and who require monitoring or general communications [S2].
  - Onion Diagram: show layers of involvement (direct users, interacting groups, organizational context, external parties) to indicate who directly interacts with the solution and who is external [S2].
  - RACI matrix: map stakeholders to major activities/requirements to indicate Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed assignments, with definition of each term to ensure consistent understanding [S5].

6. Stakeholder personas (where useful)
  - For major user groups, provide concise personas (fictional archetypes built from research) that capture main goals, behaviours, needs, and likely attitudes toward the change; note source data (interviews/surveys) used to build each persona [S5].

7. Analysis of attitudes, authority, and decision role
  - For each stakeholder/group, summarise: level of authority within the domain of change, attitude toward the change and toward business analysis, level of decision-making authority, and likely support or resistance [S1].

8. Engagement and collaboration recommendations
  - Recommended engagement level and tactics per stakeholder (e.g., collaborate regularly with high-influence/high-impact; consult SMEs; keep low-influence/low-impact informed), referencing rationale grounded in stakeholder map quadrants [S2][S3].
  - Suggested collaborative techniques to apply (workshops, collaborative games, surveys, lessons learned, risk analysis) where relevant [S3][S4].

9. Risks and mitigation related to stakeholders
  - Identify stakeholder-related risks (e.g., stakeholders diverted to other work, delayed approvals, poor elicitation quality) and recommended mitigations, drawing on BABOK guidance about monitoring and stakeholder risks [S3].

10. Traceability and links to requirements activities
  - Describe how stakeholder roles map to upcoming elicitation, requirements ownership, approvals, and RACI assignments so each requirement has identified stakeholders for validation and approval [S3][S5].

11. Review, validation and sign-off checkpoints
  - Proposed checkpoints: initial review with sponsor and PM, validation workshop with stakeholder representatives, sign-off of RACI and communications plan by sponsor or accountable owner; indicate that stakeholder engagement should be monitored and updated frequently [S2][S3].

12. Assumptions, dependencies, constraints, and exclusions
  - Example evidence-backed items to include: assumption that the initial scope identifies the impacted groups used to seed the list; dependency on availability of SMEs for interviews; constraint that stakeholder roles may change and analysis is therefore iterative [S2][S1].

13. Unresolved questions and evidence gaps
  - List missing inputs needed to finalise analysis (e.g., authoritative organizational structure, confirmed sponsor list, results of stakeholder interviews/surveys). Note: the KB does not provide specific organisation charts or stakeholder names—these must be obtained from project sources (evidence insufficient in KB for those specifics) [S1][S2].

14. Appendices
  - Raw stakeholder list export, interview notes summaries, survey results, persona research detail, RACI spreadsheet, and visual artefacts (matrix, onion diagram) used to produce the deliverable [S5].

Required source inputs and supporting evidence
- Project scope statement or solution scope to identify impacted groups [S2].
- Organizational chart or directories to identify potential stakeholders (not provided in KB; obtain from project) [S2].
- Outputs of elicitation (interviews, surveys, workshops) used to create personas and confirm attitudes [S5].
- Business objectives and future-state descriptions to focus stakeholders on common goals during engagement planning [S3].

Assumptions, dependencies and constraints (evidence-backed vs suggested placeholders)
- Evidence-backed: stakeholder analysis should start from the proposed solution scope and review who will be impacted; it is iterative and should be reviewed frequently because stakeholder positions can change [S2].
- Suggested placeholders (not specified in KB): specific timing, owners for each deliverable section, and document templates—these should be set by project governance (KB does not prescribe them).

Review, validation, approval checkpoints and owners
- Initial QA by business analyst (completeness of list and maps) and review with sponsor/executive for accuracy of influence/interest placements [S2].
- Validation workshop with representative stakeholders to confirm personas, RACI roles, and engagement approaches [S5][S3].
- Formal sign-off of stakeholder engagement approach and RACI by accountable sponsor or project manager; note that stakeholder engagement must be monitored and revised as project circumstances change [S3][S2].

Traceability and quality checks
- Link each requirement (or upcoming elicitation activity) to named stakeholders or stakeholder groups and RACI role to ensure approval/validation paths are explicit [S3][S5].
- Maintain versioned stakeholder list and visual maps; capture changes and rationale to support auditability and lessons learned [S2][S3].

Unresolved questions and evidence gaps (must be obtained from project)
- Definitive list of stakeholder individuals and contact details (not available in KB) [S2].
- Results of primary research (interviews, surveys) to support persona accuracy (KB describes persona creation but does not supply research data) [S5].
- Project-specific decisions about who is accountable for RACI sign-off and governance timings (KB describes RACI usage but does not name owners) [S5].

Notes on evidence provenance
- The outline uses BABOK guidance on stakeholder lists, maps, personas, stakeholder matrices (influence vs interest), onion diagrams, RACI matrices, iterative review, collaboration techniques, monitoring stakeholder engagement, and risks to stakeholder participation as described in the provided BABOK excerpts [S1][S2][S5][S3][S4].

If you want, I can convert this outline into a fillable template (spreadsheet + slide visuals) and draft sample content for one stakeholder group using information you provide.

question: Review the conflict between these sources about the approved requirements baseline.
knowledge_base_context:
[S1]
Title: Change Control Standard
Source: change-control-standard
Pages: 10
Retrieval rank: 1
Content:
Approved requirements baselines must be retained. Approved changes are recorded as controlled revisions so prior baselines remain auditable.

[S2]
Title: Delivery Team Procedure
Source: delivery-team-procedure
Pages: 4
Retrieval rank: 2
Content:
When a requirement change is approved, the team replaces the prior baseline file with the newly approved version.
response_text:
Directly answerable claims from supplied sources:
- Whether approved requirements baselines are retained as auditable prior versions: S1 asserts they must be retained and that approved changes are recorded as controlled revisions so prior baselines remain auditable [S1].
- Whether the prior baseline file is replaced when a requirement change is approved: S2 states the team replaces the prior baseline file with the newly approved version when a requirement change is approved [S2].

Conflict review:
1) Exact decision point in dispute
- “What happens to the prior approved requirements baseline when a change is approved — is the prior baseline retained as an auditable controlled revision, or is the prior baseline file replaced by the new version?” (disputed by S1 vs S2). 

2) Each source’s position (quoted/paraphrased separately)
- S1: “Approved requirements baselines must be retained. Approved changes are recorded as controlled revisions so prior baselines remain auditable.” This asserts retention of prior baselines and an auditable revision history [S1].
- S2: “When a requirement change is approved, the team replaces the prior baseline file with the newly approved version.” This directs replacement of the prior baseline file with the new approved version [S2].

3) Points of agreement between the sources
- Both sources address the treatment of baselines when a requirement change is approved; both recognize that an action is taken on baseline artifacts when changes are approved (S1: record controlled revisions; S2: replace file) [S1][S2].

4) Nature of the difference
- The positions are in direct operational tension: S1 requires retention of prior baselines as auditable controlled revisions, while S2 instructs replacing the prior baseline file with the newly approved version. This is a direct contradiction if taken at face value, because replacing the prior baseline file could eliminate or obscure the retained auditable prior version that S1 requires [S1][S2].
- The difference could also result from differing scope or practice (not stated in the texts). For example, S2’s “replace the prior baseline file” might be compatible with S1 if “replace” means ‘‘promote the new version as the active baseline while keeping the prior file in a revision history or archive’’ — but S2 does not state that archival or controlled revision records are kept, and S1 does not describe the file replacement process. The supplied content does not confirm whether S2’s replacement includes controlled-revision retention consistent with S1 [S1][S2].

5) Practical impact of leaving the conflict unresolved
- Risk of noncompliance with the Change Control Standard (if teams follow S2 literally and do not retain prior baselines) resulting in loss of auditable history required by S1. This could impact auditability, traceability, and regulatory or contractual obligations tied to retained baselines [S1][S2].
- Conversely, enforcing retention as S1 requires without clarifying S2’s operational steps may cause confusion for delivery teams and inconsistent document management practices [S1][S2].

6) Additional evidence or validation needed to resolve the conflict
- Clarify whether S2’s “replace the prior baseline file” includes creating/retaining a controlled revision or archive copy of the prior baseline (i.e., whether replacement is only of the active working/visible file while prior versions are stored in a revision control system or archive). The text of S2 should be expanded to state whether prior baselines are archived or recorded as controlled revisions when replacement occurs [S1][S2].
- Provide organizational guidance or examples showing the expected file-management workflow (e.g., use of version control or archive folder, naming conventions, or metadata) that demonstrates compliance with S1 while following S2’s operational step.
- Identify authoritative policy precedence (which document is the controlling standard if documents conflict) and any effective dates or scope statements that could explain applicability.

Conclusion (supported statements only):
- The two sources present conflicting operational instructions: S1 requires retention of approved baselines as auditable controlled revisions, while S2 directs replacing the prior baseline file with the newly approved version [S1][S2].
- The supplied texts do not provide enough detail to reconcile whether S2’s file replacement practice preserves the auditable prior baseline required by S1; resolving the conflict requires the clarifications listed above [S1][S2].
expected_behaviors:
- Present each source position separately.
- Identify a direct contradiction.
- Keep the conflict unresolved because authority and supersession are not established.
forbidden_behaviors:
- Blend the positions into false consensus.
- Select a governing source without evidence.

Each source’s position
- Change Control Standard: Requires that approved requirements baselines be retained and that approved changes are recorded as controlled revisions so prior baselines remain auditable. This asserts retention and auditability of prior baselines. [S1]
- Delivery Team Procedure: States that when a requirement change is approved, the team replaces the prior baseline file with the newly approved version. This asserts replacement of the prior baseline file upon approval. [S2]

- Both documents address the process that follows approval of a requirements change: both recognize that a new approved version exists after approval. [S1] [S2]

Nature of the conflict
- The difference is a direct contradiction on whether prior baselines remain retained and auditable (retain) versus being replaced (overwrite). The two positions cannot both be true for the same artifact under the same configuration management scope without additional qualifying context. [S1] [S2]

- Auditability and traceability risk: If teams follow S2 (replace file) without a separate controlled record, prior baselines may not be auditable as required by S1, exposing the organization to audit and compliance risk. [S1] [S2]
- Operational inconsistency: Different teams could implement different practices, causing confusion, loss of historical context, or failures in change-impact analysis. [S1] [S2]

The two supplied sources do not indicate authoritative precedence, tool constraints, or contextual exceptions, so obtaining that information is required to select and enforce one approach. [S1] [S2]

- There is a direct contradiction: S1 mandates retention of approved baselines; S2 mandates replacing the prior baseline file upon approval. Both positions are supported in the supplied documents and must be reconciled by clarifying authoritative policy and tool/process constraints to ensure auditability and consistent practice. [S1] [S2]
